region_of_origin: China (widely adopted across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn entry covers napa cabbage as a food item; boiled preparation is a processing state applied to this entity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code covers Chinese cabbage as a substance; boiled preparation state is not separately coded. 'closest_parent' is used as the most appropriate match type for a substance code representing the core ingredient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical side dish serving of boiled napa cabbage is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small bowl of cooked leaves served alongside a main dish.

nutrient_source.title: Boiled napa cabbage (wongbok) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for boiled napa cabbage (wongbok), CR ID F14010011. All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for boiled napa cabbage based on non-starchy leafy vegetables (GI ~15)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for boiled napa cabbage (wongbok) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy with other boiled non-starchy leafy vegetables (e.g., boiled cabbage, GI ~10–20) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021). Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (15 × 0.9) / 100 = 0.135, rounded to 0.1. This is clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (6 mg/100 g) and high potassium (221 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Boiled napa cabbage is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to its negligible sodium content (6 mg/100 g) and meaningful potassium contribution (221 mg/100 g), which supports vascular tone; avoid adding soy sauce or salt post-cooking to preserve its low-sodium benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.9 g/100 g), low GI (~15), and negligible glycaemic load (~0.1 per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Boiled napa cabbage is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its extremely low carbohydrate content (0.9 g/100 g), low estimated GI (~15), and negligible glycaemic load (~0.1) mean it has virtually no impact on postprandial blood glucose and can be consumed freely as part of a diabetes-friendly diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (1.1 g/100 g), and potassium (221 mg/100 g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Boiled napa cabbage is cardioprotective: it contains no fat, no cholesterol, negligible sodium, and provides dietary fibre and potassium that support healthy blood pressure and lipid profiles; it is suitable for daily inclusion in a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cruciferous vegetable with anti-inflammatory phytonutrients (sulforaphane precursors, antioxidants) and negligible saturated fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Boiled napa cabbage is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory cruciferous phytonutrients and absence of pro-inflammatory saturated fat; avoid pickled or fermented versions (e.g., kimchi) if on a sodium-restricted diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled napa cabbage (wongbok) is a minimally processed leafy vegetable dish prepared by boiling fresh napa cabbage (Brassica rapa subsp. pekinensis) in water until tender. The result is pale green-white, soft leaves with a mild, slightly sweet flavour and a delicate, tender texture. Napa cabbage — also known as wongbok, wombok, Chinese cabbage, or pe-tsai — is widely consumed across East and Southeast Asia, including Singapore, China, Korea, and Japan, where it is a staple vegetable used in soups, stir-fries, hot pots, and as a side dish. Nutritionally, boiled napa cabbage is extremely low in calories (11 kcal/100 g), very low in carbohydrates (0.9 g/100 g), and contains no fat and no cholesterol. It provides 1.1 g protein, 1.1 g dietary fibre, 6 mg sodium, and a notable 221 mg potassium per 100 g. Its very low sodium and high potassium content make it ideal for DASH-diet adherence and blood pressure management. The negligible carbohydrate content and low estimated glycaemic index (~15) render it essentially glycaemia-neutral, making it an excellent choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The absence of fat and cholesterol, combined with dietary fibre and potassium, supports cardiovascular health. As a cruciferous vegetable, napa cabbage contains sulforaphane precursors and antioxidants with anti-inflammatory properties relevant to conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. It is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Data sourced from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F14010011).

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F14010011) at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. The food identity is unambiguous. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy leafy vegetables, which is the approved GI source.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F14010011): boiled napa cabbage contains no fat by its natural composition — confirmed zero. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero as a consequence of zero total fat — confirmed zero. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F14010011) — confirmed zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this food. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F14010011; USDA FoodData Central (NDB 11119, Chinese cabbage cooked) provides some of these values but has not been merged here to preserve source integrity of the HPB canonical record. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on boiled non-starchy leafy vegetables from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for boiled napa cabbage (wongbok) is available in the database as of 2026-04-16. Glycaemic load per serving (100 g) = (15 × 0.9) / 100 ≈ 0.1, which is clinically negligible. The input record listed 'keto' as a dietary tag; this is supported by the extremely low carbohydrate content (0.9 g/100 g). Multilingual names provided for Singaporean cuisine context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi). Ontology: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 is the napa cabbage food entity; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for Chinese cabbage (pe-tsai) boiled; SNOMED CT 226723005 is an approximate match for Chinese cabbage as a substance, with match_type set to 'closest_parent' as it represents the core ingredient.
